speaker: interviewer
text: This is Leah [? Schiffern ?] Averick interviewing Lou Joseph on December 17, 1996, tape three, in Des Plaines, Illinois.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I'd like to come back to that comment that I made earlier about when the clinic was totally bombed out and the building was destroyed and were maybe about 30 or 40 casualties among the patients. And but I think this governess-- her name was Marie-- she was the only casualty from the staff that was-- she was in the elevator. Anyway, I was trying to-- you know, I tried to stand guard at the beginning at the entrance to the clinic and put on that air warden's helmet.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after a while, my mother came over and says, take it off. Put your regular cap on and hat on because you look like a member of the Jewish brigade. I think what she meant was that she-- I'm in the wrong place at the wrong time looking like I am, and that I might provoke somebody and get somebody thinking that, you know, what's this? He looks so Jewish. That was even more expressed my, my Jewish looks by enhancing my Jewish looks by having that helmet on. And so that's-- that was not a good thing to do at this time. Could you explain what you mean by Jewish looks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my, my, my looks, I mean, my face. Because people were identified by their looks a lot a times being as Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your eyes? Dark--

speaker: interviewee
text: Could be eyes, hair, could be nose, could be anything. But there's a certain look about some-- and it's not always true, I mean. A lot of people of other races, of other backgrounds, a lot of Italians, sometimes Greeks, could also be--

speaker: interviewer
text: Arabs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Arabs too, identified as Jews. But at that particular time, that's what she meant. She did not want, want me to cause any attention, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Because what might happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that somebody might take offense to it and say, why-- you know, what is he doing? What-- well, why is he-- why isn't he in the army, or why isn't he working somewhere, you know? And I was, you know, kicked out of the school. I was doing this job with this person, you know, in the-- in, in this insurance office. And that, boy, really was like just a, a farce. That was just, you know, it was not really a job, and everybody knew it was just pretend.

speaker: interviewer
text: Work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pretend work, you know? There was not no real work. So that was-- I think, was the reason why she said that, not to-- and, you know-- instigate any attention by-- draw any attention by somebody else, you know, because everybody was so immersed in their own problems with the air raids. People were-- you know, buildings were destroyed. They were still burning, and, and many people were killed, you know? So it didn't seem like a good time to bring the-- bring up this issue, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: They might take--?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, might take offense to it, you know, and say-- you know, it was just not-- it was just not smart.

speaker: interviewer
text: Safe for you. It would not say sale. Would you agree?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah-- would not-- no, it would not be safe because somebody might start asking questions. And we may have a problem answering them, you know? So coming back to the-- after the-- our apartment was bombed out in November, which was a terrible air raid--

speaker: interviewer
text: November 1943. Is that right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. November 22, 1943 was a saturation air raid where they systematically bombed a whole-- certain quarters of the city. And just everything within that-- within those limits were totally destroyed with incendiary-- blockbuster bombs and then incendiary bombs. So then everything started burning down. In fact, the fire was so that you couldn't breathe anymore. The oxygen became sparse, and it just fueled more fires. We went to a high rise bunker where there was-- near the zoological garden in Berlin. And there was like 10 stories high or maybe higher, and that was like a refuge to get there for all the people that were bombed out. We stayed there for about two nights. They always had panic in that bunker too because they had anti-aircraft batteries stationed on top of that. And the bombers hit the-- they hit the bunker. They couldn't penetrate them. They couldn't penetrate. There was such a strong cement building, you know, that you couldn't penetrate it. But they did hit the batteries. And the vibration of the-- even, you know, down on the ground floor was bad enough so that people almost started to become panicky and started to rush towards the exits of the-- there was-- of the bunker and started to almost being trampled upon and people falling down. And I noticed that the German Air Force soldiers that were part of this aircraft battery, they came out with their pistols drawn and trying to keep the crowd back from panicking anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then the next day, we went to-- there were still trains going out of Berlin, although most of the train stations were bombed out. And we-- we went to Silesia, which is now part of Poland, because we had friends there. And we were-- and tried to stay there. What did you use for money? How did you travel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we had money. We had money because after, after the clinic was bombed out, we got a commendation for by the-- by the government fellow amount that was pretty much equal in the income that we had, of the clinic had. That was part of the-- oh yeah. The money wasn't worth anything, really. I mean, you couldn't do-- everything was rationed, goods. But you still needed money. So you couldn't buy anything extraordinary, any luxury, you just had your bare-- food was all-- was, you know, severely rationed, clothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had money, so we got-- went to Silesia and with those friends. I immediately signed up the-- filled out the forms at the local police department in that community. And a few days later, I got a summons to report to work to a big factory that was making-- refurbishing big steam rollers, putting a rubber-- a layer of rubber-- levels of rubber on these steam rollers, which were then used in other factories to make clothes out of wood. It was actually-- an actual-- an actual like way that they made-- that the German army made uniforms for their people, out of wood, because they had no wool.

speaker: interviewee
text: Germany was totally cut off by the Naval blockade by all the Allied troops, so wool-- there wasn't very much wool production in Germany. So they needed uniforms, so they made. They had a liquid, and made it into a liquid mass, the wood. And then they would somehow, through a chemicals process, they would convert this into, into fabrics. They were very stiff, and I think people scratched a lot, but, [LAUGHS] but that was all right. Anyway, we were-- the factory wasn't making those uniforms. They were making the tools for it, those big steamrollers. It was very dangerous, very hard labor for me. And I had to travel through on a bicycle. There was no public transportation. And I had to be there. I mean, not showing up there would have meant a lot of-- a lot of serious problems. So I was in the wintertime all bundled up, half frozen, half-- complete almost totally stiff. Take me half an hour before I thaw off in the-- in the locker room, change to the work clothes, you know. And doing this, it was dangerous work because I almost get killed. I'm not familiar. I worked with among regular laborers. These will mostly older men, because all the young men were in the German army. And they were not-- they didn't even know who I was. They probably wondered why I was there because young. And I told them that I was not-- I had not been physically acceptable-- that's what I used-- for the German army. I wasn't going to say or tell them the real story. So they accepted that. And they were actually nice to me. In fact, one of them practically saved my life when one of those big rollers was-- they weighed like three, four tons, and they were on these overhead cranes that they were moving across the aisles. And I was in the way, and I didn't even notice it. I'm not-- I'm not familiar with factory work and this type of work. So at the last second, this thing would have crushed me if this roller, as it was going by. He pulled me into, into a corner, you know, out of the way. And so after that, I thought I can't do this work. And this is not fair that they're doing this. I have a hernia. I'm not physically able to do this kind of work. So I went back to the employment. I requested that I get another interview with the local employment office and a medical examination.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to this-- to the doctor there, and he said, no, you're not-- he said, I don't know who assigned you to this work, but you're not capable of doing that. And I'm going to give you a different-- everybody has to work. There was no question. So he assigned me a different job in a factory. They were making radios for German fighter planes. Where was this, and what was the name of the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the same area there. In the same vicinity. It was a small factory, had about 30, 40 women-- all women, there was no men. I don't even think the-- maybe the, the foreman was a man. Everybody else was women. They were all married women. They're all, all the women-- this was not-- not just I had to work as a Mischling. Everybody in Germany was needing all the resources, human resources, that, that they could get. So everybody was required to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these women were all there. And I could not believe. I'm not very good in with fixing and making things. So here I was suddenly assigned to put intricate little radios together for fighter planes, having no idea. And what I couldn't believe is that they have no quality control. Nobody checked those radios as they were going out. I didn't put-- I mean, I didn't do it purposely. I mean, I was just sloppy in a way. Maybe with some ulterior motive because anything I do helps German fighter planes, then that means that the war is going to be prolonged. And the longer the war goes on, the lesser my chances to survive. You already knew that, that you might not survive as a Mischling?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew that because people were telling us, you know, combination, even in the Nazi propaganda press, you know, they were saying that they were-- that they were also taking sharp, very sharp, actions against people that were not just full Jewish. They were Mischling and so forth. So that was known. Everybody knew that. I mean, it started with kicking you out of school, and then-- excuse me-- and then denying you any opportunity to become a professional anything. You couldn't do-- you were a non-existing person.

speaker: interviewer
text: What word did they use, non-existing person?

speaker: interviewee
text: Untermenschen. That's the word that they use for any-- they used it for gypsies too, and for homosexuals and so forth, although they had homosexuals in the Nazi hierarchy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hierarchy, there was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they did. Yeah. But some of them got killed. They murdered them too, once they-- you know, once they got fed up with them or so. But they had, yeah.  We had them later on in the camp. We had people like this too, unfortunately.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean homosexuals?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know them, but I heard from later on in the camp when I was-- yeah, I heard that they were. And unfortunately, they were like the trustees. I mean, they were sent in by the-- some of them were, were put in charge of by some of the guards, you know? I don't know why, if there was any reason for that. But coming back to the-- that-- the medical examination where the doctor assigned me to the-- I was in that factory, working with 40 women. It was very difficult because they all wondered why I wasn't on the-- on the Russian front. That was, you know. So again, I had to use-- I was not acceptable for physical reasons. I can only do light work. So that went over. I did not tell them anything about me.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were all German women you were working with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother have to go to work as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she, she was-- she didn't have to go, no. I don't know. They might, might have put her-- excuse me. They might have put her, but she didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was still too young. For some reason they--

speaker: interviewer
text: So did he go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he wasn't doing anything. He was just sitting around. He was not doing anything. He couldn't go to school either. He couldn't enroll in school in that new community either. I mean, there was no-- no. He was banned from that. But he just stayed home, just stayed with my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I was the one that got all-- had to take all the brunt of this whole situation. So I was with all these women, you know. And these-- it was actually difficult with those women. You have a-- what, 17, 16-- '44-- I'm 17 years old, you know? And all these women without their boyfriends and, and husbands-- they're all on the front. And I have to keep my-- that would have been the last thing I would be interested in. What do you mean? They were making overtures to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they were interested. Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: In sexual relations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, more or less, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't-- I mean, I was a-- I was a teenager, really, you know? But still, they were looking for any male that would have been-- anybody that came along. But I really wasn't interested. That was the last-- it would've been the last thing on my mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why? What kept you? What prevented you from--

speaker: interviewee
text: If they found out who I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: That is, you are a--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I would-- they could go and report me to the police, to the authorities, and I would be immediately arrested because that's what they call Rassenschande.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that called?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rassenschande. That means you, you insult the, the German race.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you were a half Jew, a Mischling?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. If you-- yeah. If you ever dared to have relations with somebody, oh yeah. No, no, that was-- that was clear. That was the law. That was the-- for people like myself. You have no-- you stay away from-- you're prohibited from even looking at German women, not to say about anything else.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean by anything else?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, can't get involved in relations with them, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- not that I-- that I desired that, you know, because I knew every day-- like I said before, I knew that they were trying to-- that our-- that our situation was pretty bad and almost hopeless, you know? And every day that we survived was another day-- another day gained in and our lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were aware that you, as a Mischling, were in a precarious situation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very precarious situation. It was-- I was-- this, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: Even in Silesia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, everywhere. Everywhere in Germany, or in German-occupied territories, sure. You could not. You could not. You were an outcast. You were not-- you were not supposed to have any, any-- not even just talking, really. Well, I had to talk because, you know, I worked in a factory. There were lunch breaks and so forth. You did talk, but I never said anything about what my background was, never.

speaker: interviewer
text: I just said-- when they asked me, how come you're not in the army? I said, well, I was. I even lied. I said, but they rejected me because they sent me back here because that was before computers. Who's going to go and-- who's going to go and try to find out, you know? Today, you couldn't do these things anymore. They'd punch your Social Security number in the computer, and they'd know everything about you. So you were that terrified?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Oh yeah. But I still-- I wasn't on unfriendly terms with these people. They were not hostile to me. And those women-- except that I thought they couldn't do the work, you know? They were not-- they were-- I mean, this is like trying to buy-- to build a Boeing 757 with people like myself, you know? That would be-- they're no-- they're no experts in this work. They were just pulled together, like they were so short on, on manpower, that they pulled anybody in. They must have so the sloppiest work. I saw some of these things going, standing on the-- on the-- on the dock or on the shelves ready for shipment to the front that I couldn't believe in what condition they were.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you didn't speak up.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't speak up. And I wanted to do that. I wanted to see them bad in bad shape because remember the Messerschmidt pilot trying to attack a spitfire, you know, and suddenly he's trying to call his buddy on the phone-- on the radio and the radio doesn't work? Well, that was our fault. So that's, that's what happened. Then the next step was, I thought I could do this. I could do sloppy work. I mean, I didn't, you know, deliberately. It was difficult to do it because they-- you know. But I could-- sloppy, you could always excuse it. I don't know, playing dumb. So I passed it on. But I knew my work was terrible. I mean, probably none of those radios that I ever worked on was in working order when they needed it. But anyway, and I never thought about it until one day when the mail came, and I came home from work from the factory, my mother's all upset. She says, you've got a summons from the Gestapo in the-- in the county seat, in the city where the counties--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was all the committees, community. Said to appear in 10 days to for, for a hearing. Reason, defense sabotage. Now, I figured right away, somebody must have said something, must have tracked-- traced back a-- traced back a radio to this factory. And they probably said, hey, this guy's working. He's an enemy because he's a Mischling, you know? So that's how somebody reported me to the Gestapo, probably the administrator of that factory and the plant director.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- my mother said, no, way. We're not going to go. We're going to skip. We're going to flee. And we walked a couple nights later. We left everything there. We didn't take any suitcases. We took just a handbag like we're going out for the evening, and we walked for five miles. We didn't even go use the train station in that community in fear that somebody was already watching us. We walked at night for five and then caught the midnight train out of the next community back to Berlin.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in Berlin, we went to the-- the next day, we went to the police department-- no, the housing bureau, and we got a-- because we needed-- we needed to get an apartment. They put us in an apartment. There were so many empty apartments, the buildings that were not destroyed from the bombing raids. They put us in an apartment that was occupied by some high government official of the Propaganda Ministry, some baron. He was an aristocrat. But he wasn't there. He and his wife had fled to their country estate because of the bombings. But that didn't prevent that he had to give up his-- yeah, because they had such a-- such a, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: Shortage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shortage of houses, you know? So they-- so they had to give-- make their apartment available. That was a really great apartment, a luxury apartment. All the furniture in there so it was-- so we didn't need anything. And we just had to get some more clothes. So but then the next they, after the housing, the next step was to go to the police department, and again, sign that form.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had to? There was no way of avoiding it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because I was afraid that the Gestapo-- we wanted to give them a plausible reason why we didn't show up for that-- for that hearing. We had avoided that hearing. And we would say we had a family emergency, just make something up, you know? But to stay on the right track, to keep everything legal, we wanted-- we went to the police department. That was a totally different suburb we had never been in of Berlin, so nobody knew us. But you still had to put in the form who I was and who my brother was.

speaker: interviewer
text: That you were a--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, a Mischling, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is it Mischling-1, Mischling-2? How did they-- how did you write it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, just. I told you, half Aryan, [GERMAN] That means, means, you know, one parent was not-- was Jewish and one not. So we went there, and we did. And we were in that apartment for a while. And there was-- I'm trying to think. It was only maybe for a month. And I hadn't even-- and I had registered with the employment office again, but I had not done anything. But they hadn't called me, so then my-- then we got really worried that we did not go to the Gestapo hearing. And that whatever it is, that by not-- by not showing up there may make things worse.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we decided tomorrow we're going to go to the Gestapo headquarters, which was notorious for once people enter that building-- it's the big headquarters. That's the national headquarters for all the Gestapo activities in Berlin. Once they entered, most of the people don't ever come out again. So that-- that had that reputation. So what we decided, the next day, we're going to go there, my mother and I, take that summons with us and say, we had a family emergency. We couldn't make it. But we're coming now and trying to clarify, to clear up this case. So we go there, and it happens to be July 20, 1944. It's the day that the-- a group of high ranking army officers, German army officers, assassinate Hitler, planting a bomb in a briefcase in his Wolf's Lair headquarters outside Berlin.

speaker: interviewee
text: And but the bomb goes off, but Hitler has just either walked out of the room or to the other end of the room. Somebody else gets killed, but Hitler doesn't get killed. And of course, that defeats the whole-- the whole plot. Nothing is-- we didn't know that. We didn't know that. We're coming in, go into the headquarters, reporting. You go in through guards, check in through the guards, show them the summons, go upstairs.

speaker: interviewer
text: He says-- every, every door has-- it's like a prison here, you know? It's like gates, you know, steel gates. And you-- guards standing around. Sending us up to a room where there's an older police officer in, in civilian clothes. Turns out, when I show him the summons, he's totally speechless. He doesn't he's-- doesn't know what to-- how to react to it.

speaker: interviewee
text: It turns out that he is not a member of the Gestapo. He is a regular homicide or police officer, a detective, that they pulled in because all the henchmen are out executing the-- they executed scores of them of all the-- over at the headquarters, army headquarters in Berlin, executing all the ones that they suspected of being part of that assassination plot of Hitler. That was my luck, because if they had been a regular guy, he goes over to some file cabinet, picks my summons, looking for my name. Of course, that was, like I said, before computers. So they never communicated this incident that we didn't show up, the Gestapo in this other-- in Silesia. So we did-- he, he can't find anything, so he hands me back the summons. And he says, I can't find anything. You did the right thing. You came back, and you reported. [LAUGHS] And if we want you, I'm sure they're going to contact you again. So never anything happened. We got out of that building. I don't know how thankful I was going through all those gates and guards, guard checkpoints, and getting out of there. My mother and I looked at us. We couldn't understand why because we hadn't heard about it. We hadn't heard that. That was in the morning. Then later on the German radio-- of course, the Nazi radio distorted everything and said, you know, some traitors, a group of traitors has tried to assassinate the Fuhrer, but God has protected him. He didn't believe in God, really. So but the superior powers have protected the Fuhrer, and all those--

speaker: interviewer
text: Traitors.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, traitors will all be severely punished. They will all be executed, every one of them. Some were hung up on meat hooks. It was-- and Hitler had taken a film of them, and he wanted to see that. He had it shown to him later so he could have the satisfaction to see that they were all killed. I mean, that's pretty, pretty awful to people. I mean, they could have shot them or could have hanged them, but they actually hung them up on meat hooks. That was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. There were people on the-- so that's, that ended that period. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the meantime, you're not working.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not working. And now I'm waiting for an assignment from the employment department because I can't think-- everybody-- I was in forced labor in the factory in Silesia. How did they miss me? Well, they didn't miss me because in October, I get another summons. This time it says, you are to report for embarkation at the Grunewald station, railroad station, for shipment to the-- to a-- I don't think they said forced labor camp. They said, to a camp. I don't think they said anything about the camp, about the nature of the camp. And to report there at 7 o'clock. And so that's what I-- you know, that's what I did. I, I had no idea what was going to happen. All I know is, when I get to the camp-- to the railroad station-- there's 500 other men of all ages there, everyone with suitcases and dressed in civilian clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You go there alone?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I went my family. My mother and brother took me, yeah, to see me off. And they thought, maybe it's just a harmless, you know, assignment, probably of forced labor, but not-- you know, not-- little idea did we have of what would happen, I mean. Everything was very harmless when we were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were where?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the-- at the railroad station. But we were in regular railroad cars. We were not in cattle cars. And the-- actually, the terrible-- the seriousness of the situation did not become visible until we get to our destination, which was near Zerbst, which is in the German-- near the German-- larger German city of Magdeburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
